---
title: "From Nutella on toast to statistical models"
format:
  html:
    df-print: paged
---

## Introduction

Imagine a piece of toast that’s come out of the toaster with a faint grid pattern pressed in by the rack. We’ll treat each little square in that pattern as a possible outcome in our sample space. Next, we spread 1 Kg of Nutella across the toast so that some squares get a thick coating and others barely any at all. 

Wherever the Nutella is **thick**, the probability is **high**; where it’s **thin or absent**, the probability is **low** or **zero**. But taken together, the **total** Nutella spread must sum to 1 Kg.  

In this notebook, we’ll model several probability distributions/stastistical models as different ways of smearing Nutella across the toast.

![](images/jar-nutella-chocolate-spread-bread-niedersachsen-germany-september-glass-ferrero-slice-white-background-91085621.jpg)

## Setup environment

```{r}
#| output: false
library(tidyverse)
library(ggformula)
library(extraDistr)

theme_set(theme_minimal())

set.seed(666)
```

## Helper functions

```{r}
# Plot probability distribution
plot_probability_distribution <- function(df, xvar = "X", yvar = "Y", pvar = "p", title = "p(X, Y)", label_digits = 3) {
  
  tile_formula <- as.formula(paste0(yvar, " ~ ", xvar))
  
  label_expr <- lazyeval::interp(
    ~ sprintf(paste0("%0.", label_digits, "f"), var),
    var = as.name(pvar)
  )
  
  plot_obj <- gf_tile(
    tile_formula,
    data = df,
    fill = as.formula(paste0("~ ", pvar)),
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.2
  ) %>%
    gf_labs(title = title) %>%
    gf_text(label = label_expr, size = 2) %>%
    gf_refine(
      scale_fill_gradientn(
        colors = c("white","chocolate4","black"),
        values = c(0, 0.1, 1),
        limits = c(0, 1)
      ),
      scale_x_continuous(breaks = unique(df[[xvar]])),
      scale_y_continuous(breaks = unique(df[[yvar]]))
    )
  
  return(plot_obj)
}

# Plot random samples from probability distribution
plot_random_samples <- function(df, n, xvar = "X", yvar = "Y", pvar = "p", title = "p(X, Y)") {
  df_sampled <- df %>%
    slice_sample(n = n, weight_by = .[[pvar]], replace = TRUE)
  
  plot_obj <- gf_point(
    as.formula(paste0(yvar, " ~ ", xvar)),
    data = df_sampled,
    alpha = 0.4,
    size = 4,
    color = "chocolate4",
    stroke = 0
  ) %>%
    gf_refine(
      scale_x_continuous(breaks = unique(df[[xvar]]), minor_breaks = NULL),
      scale_y_continuous(breaks = unique(df[[yvar]]), minor_breaks = NULL)
    )
  
  return(plot_obj)
}
```

## Simulate data from uniform and linear models

```{r}
n <- 50
sample.size <- 10
grid.size <- 10
```

```{r}
X <- seq(0, grid.size, length.out = grid.size + 1)
Y <- seq(0, grid.size, length.out = grid.size + 1)

ss <-
  expand_grid(X, Y) %>%
  mutate(Ω = paste0("⍵", 1:n())) %>%
  select(Ω, everything())

ss
```

```{r}
ss %>% 
  mutate(p = 1 / n()) %>%
  plot_probability_distribution()
```

```{r}
ss %>% 
  mutate(p = 1 / n()) %>%
  plot_random_samples(n)
```

```{r}
ss %>% 
  mutate(p = 1 / n()) %>%
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate(E = (Y == X)) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(p = if_else(E, p / sum(p[E]), 0)) %>%
  plot_probability_distribution()
```



```{r}
ss %>% 
  mutate(p = 1 / n()) %>%
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate(E = (Y == X)) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(p = if_else(E, p / sum(p[E]), 0)) %>%
  plot_random_samples(n)
```

```{r}
ss %>% 
  mutate(p = 1 / n()) %>%
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate(E = (Y == X + 0) | (Y == (X + 1)) | (Y == (X - 1))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(p = if_else(E, p / sum(p[E]), 0)) %>%
  plot_probability_distribution()
```



```{r}
ss %>% 
  mutate(p = 1 / n()) %>%
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate(E = (Y == X + 0) | (Y == (X + 1)) | (Y == (X - 1))) %>% 
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(p = if_else(E, p / sum(p[E]), 0)) %>%
  plot_random_samples(n)
```



```{r}
ss %>% 
  mutate(p = 1 / n()) %>%
  mutate(p = ddnorm(Y, mean = X, sd = 1), p = p / sum(p)) %>%
  plot_probability_distribution()
```

```{r}
ss %>% 
  mutate(p = 1 / n()) %>%
  mutate(p = ddnorm(Y, mean = X, sd = 1)) %>%
  plot_random_samples(n)
```
